text.title: Business Entity and Essential Policies
text.summary: Entity selection framework and required online-business policies for a new solopreneur product.

section.title: Entity Choice

rule.statement: Form a business entity before taking real money. Personal liability flowing from business activities is the most common and most preventable founder mistake.
rule.rationale: Sole proprietorships provide zero liability protection. An LLC costs $50–500 to form and creates a legal barrier between business debts and personal assets.

list.item: Sole Proprietorship — no liability protection, personal taxes, use only for idea validation
LLC — liability protection, pass-through taxes, credible with customers; correct choice for most solopreneurs
S-Corp — liability protection, salary + distributions tax structure; evaluate at $80–100K+ SE income
C-Corp — liability protection, corporate taxes; only for investment-seeking startups

rule.statement: For US-based businesses, form in home state unless Delaware or Wyoming offers a specific advantage (e.g., anonymous ownership in Wyoming, or investor expectation in Delaware for future fundraising).
rule.rationale: Foreign-state registration requires a registered agent fee plus an extra annual report in your home state, negating cost savings for most solopreneurs.

section.p: LLC formation steps: choose state, file articles of organization, obtain EIN from IRS, open business bank account, register for state taxes if required.

section.title: Required Policies

rule.statement: Publish a Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, Cookie Policy, and Refund Policy before accepting user data or payment from any jurisdiction.
rule.rationale: GDPR requires a Privacy Policy as a legal obligation for any EU user; ToS limits liability; Cookie Policy is required for GDPR/CCPA cookie consent.

section.p: Terms of Service must include: acceptance of terms, service description, user responsibilities, payment terms, IP ownership, disclaimers (no warranty, limitation of liability), termination, and governing law.

Privacy Policy must include: data collected and how, purpose of use, third-party sharing, retention period, user rights (access/correction/deletion, GDPR/CCPA), security measures, and contact information.
section.reference: Required sections for ToS and Privacy Policy as plain-text scaffolds

rule.statement: Never copy a competitor's policy. Copyright violation and factual inaccuracies from the copied policy both create enforcement risks.
rule.rationale: Policies contain factual claims about your data practices. A competitor's claims about their vendors and retention do not match yours.

rule.statement: Tag and date every policy at the top with "Effective Date: YYYY-MM-DD, Version: N" and maintain a version history page.
rule.rationale: Regulators and users reference specific effective dates during disputes; un-dated policies have no evidentiary force.
